metadata.core_positioning: SpeedTrac addresses the problem that traditional speed training (SuperSpeed, Stack, Rypstick) makes slices/hooks WORSE because they use on-axis weight positioning with no face control feedback.

hated_features.feature: No face control feedback during speed training
quote_evidence: I gained 15 mph with SuperSpeed but my slice got worse. Now I'm hitting it farther into the trees than before.
Every speed training session without face control is literally practice making your bad habits permanent at higher speeds.
Started practicing with speed stik got speed up to 100 mph. But starting spraying ball left, right, and everywhere.

It definitely works. I'm scratch and had 108 chs. I got up to 117 with the training in just a few weeks. However, in my case, I became flippy. I found I couldn't maintain my drive and hold golf swing that was mainly pivot driven. I developed way too much flip/roll release to generate the speed. So I stopped the training bc my accuracy fell off badly.
hated_features.contrast_opportunity: SpeedTrac's integrated feedback shows clubface angle during every swing, training speed AND accuracy simultaneously

hated_features.feature: Speed gains don't transfer to the course
quote_evidence: I've been doing SuperSpeed for 6 months and my driving distance on the course is the same. Radar says I gained speed but the ball doesn't go any farther.

I personally was a proponent of Stack. Through using it, I saw fairly significant improvements in my numbers on Stack. I stress that only to say that I found that the crossover of benefits of Stack training to my actual on course speeds hit a plateau very quickly.

The initial neuromuscular changes that Stack drives just from an intent standpoint added a quick ~5 mph of head speed to my driver, that I would say generally came with me to the golf course. However, I never saw much change in iron head speeds, and I could not seem to progress beyond those initial gains when I was actually playing golf, despite over two years of pretty consistent Stack training

For all you guys using speed training tools, does it readily convert to the course? I'm 59, fairly fit and I know can swing faster than I do on the tee, proven by testing inside with GC Quad, but when I stand on the tee with the risk of a bad outcome, I am more focussed and feel I swing at maybe 85-90%.
hated_features.contrast_opportunity: SpeedTrac trains the exact motor pattern you need on the course - fast swings with squared face

hated_features.feature: Training stick length doesn't match driver
quote_evidence: The speed sticks are shorter than a driver at 42 inches vs 45 inches. It's closer to 7-iron length. How is that supposed to transfer?

The second reason I think Rypstick is more beneficial is that it's the length of a driver. While the Stack System is 42 inches, closer to a 7-iron. I've never quite understood why The Stack is so much shorter than a standard length driver.
hated_features.contrast_opportunity: SpeedTrac's driver-length design ensures 1:1 transfer to your actual swing

quote_evidence: The Stack app is complicated and took time to learn. Felt like I needed a PhD just to do speed training.

Does anyone know if I could use The Stack app with Superspeed sticks? I have the Superspeed sticks already and a PRGR radar but I'm getting very frustrated with Superspeed for not having an app in this day and age.
hated_features.contrast_opportunity: SpeedTrac provides instant visual feedback - no apps, no complex protocols

quote_evidence: The Stack System costs $299 plus $230 for the radar plus $100/year subscription. That's over $600 just to start speed training.
Requires additional purchase of compatible launch monitor. Another $230 I wasn't expecting to spend.
Need to purchase speed measurement device separately. That's another $150-230 on top of the training system.
Ongoing subscription cost after first year. $100/year just to access training protocols I already learned.
hated_features.contrast_opportunity: SpeedTrac is all-in-one - no radar needed, no subscriptions, no hidden costs

quote_evidence: Takes up too much space in golf bag. I can't fit all three sticks plus my clubs.
My SuperSpeed Stick experience is that it works if you work it but I think I would prefer a one stick system.
At the end of the day, Stack is better than SuperSpeed because it's one club vs three
hated_features.contrast_opportunity: SpeedTrac is a single, complete training system

hate_quotes: I gained 15 mph with SuperSpeed but my slice got worse. Now I'm hitting it farther into the trees than before.
I've been doing SuperSpeed for 6 months and my driving distance on the course is the same.
Super Speed caused some physical injuries for me.
SSG makes their money from selling sticks, and they make more money from selling three sticks than from selling one.
Ive posted in the ss thread that Ive had just as good results without any sticks or program, just using common sense. But its a bit of a cult over there and they don't seem to appreciate my input.
hated_products.speedtrac_counter: SpeedTrac trains face control WITH speed, preventing slice amplification

hate_quotes: The Stack System costs $299 plus $230 for the radar plus $100/year subscription. That's over $600 just to start speed training.

Was using Stack and herniated S1/L5 disc 3.5 years ago.
The Stack is one club that is about the length of a 7-iron... However, it's a lot for most golfers and somewhat of a steep learning curve. Not to mention the steep price tag.
Nearly finished 2nd sessions with Stack. But disappointingly stalled on 102mph for the last 2 months.
specific_complaints: Short length doesn't transfer to driver
hated_products.speedtrac_counter: SpeedTrac is all-in-one at a fraction of the total cost with instant visual feedback

hate_quotes: The first stick did not work at all so I complained and they sent a replacement. This lasted about 4 days and then the sliding part cracked and broke. In my opinion it was a total waste of money
In the beginning it was fine but the speed gauge stopped working. Called customer service and was basically told sorry for your luck. So to me the $125 i spent was wasted money.
Started practicing with speed stik got speed up to 100 mph. But starting spraying ball left, right, and everywhere.
the first one the slide started getting stuck, I called the sent a new one, the second one the slide cracked
hated_products.speedtrac_counter: SpeedTrac is built to last and trains both speed AND directional control

hate_quotes: I have tried a weighted driver did not help hurt my back from it. Making me over turn because of the weight.
momentus(sucks)
SuperSpeed found that swinging an object over about 5% heavier than a standard driver can actually train that motor pattern to move slower.
hated_products.speedtrac_counter: SpeedTrac uses overspeed principles with face control feedback

hate_quotes: Most training aids are trash
I have purchased or tried out nearly all the modern day training aids. I heartily endorse none of them. Waste of money.
So to those training aids which 'force' your body into a certain position, those don't work
Golfers who want to believe some training contraption will fix their swing are gullible
Others? Overhyped gimmicks that end up collecting dust in the garage.
hated_products.speedtrac_counter: SpeedTrac delivers measurable results with built-in accountability through face control feedback

hate_quotes: I can't believe you used that 2nd video. LMAO. Everybody and their mothers know that it's not possible to gain 80 YARDS from a couple little fixes.
Those videos hook us with their quick-fix magic tricks, but they rarely deliver the goods when we hit the course.
hated_messaging.contrast_messaging: SpeedTrac requires consistent training but delivers USABLE gains because it trains accuracy with speed

hate_quotes: Rule of thumb is 3 yards more for every 1 mph increase. Personally I've never seen that. I think the rule of thumb is based on perfect launch and spin and dead center impact.
But here is the problem, I was hitting my 7 iron 140-145, and my driver 235. I still can't hit any farther. The release of the club(s) is not the same or I must be doing something wrong.
hated_messaging.contrast_messaging: Speed only equals distance when you can CONTROL where the ball goes. SpeedTrac trains both.

hate_quotes: Eddie being able to crush it doesn't necessarily mean he is the ideal guy to teach other folks to swing faster.
this kinda feels like everyone thinking the biggest guy in the gym or the best golfer has the best advice which aren't always or even usually true.
hated_messaging.contrast_messaging: SpeedTrac works for real golfers with real swing faults

quote_evidence: Used the SuperSpeed and followed the training protocol strictly for about two months. My swing speed definitely increased. A good hit of my driver goes 10-15 yards further. However, the biggest problem is it will break your tempo and rhythm since you will try to swing and hit as hard as possible. I drive the ball further on good contacts, but my bad contacts also increase dramatically. By the end of day, my score suffers.

do they work, yes they do, for the better not always...I played to a 11 15 years ago playing from a open stance and hitting every club with a little fade...then on vacation I saw an add for a 'swing Magic club'...first shot a nine iron on a short par 3, first hook I had seen in years, right out of bounds
15 years later still cannot find that fade I had and cannot get down from a 16
hated_experiences.contrast_opportunity: SpeedTrac maintains your natural shot shape while increasing speed

quote_evidence: I gained 15 mph with SuperSpeed but my slice got worse. Now I'm hitting it farther into the trees than before.
Why can't I take a full swing on the driver without slicing? If I take a lot slower swing it goes straight(about 250 yards) but when I swing faster it slices. The harder I swing the more it slices.
hated_experiences.contrast_opportunity: SpeedTrac ensures your speed gains translate to FAIRWAYS, not tree lines

Nearly finished 2nd sessions with Stack. But disappointingly stalled on 102mph for the last 2 months.

My back has been sore ever since I started overspeed training. Not sure it's worth the extra yards.
I'm 55 years old and afraid I'm going to hurt myself swinging these sticks as fast as I can. Where's the warm-up protocol?
Swinging a weighted club is fine, but swinging it as fast as you possibly can, can lead to injury.

objections.objection: Speed training aids don't really work
how_they_express_it: Most training aids are trash
Swing Trainers gimmick or good
Are the many golf trainers for sale, David Leadbetter, Momentus etc etc a waste of money??
I have purchased or tried out nearly all the modern day training aids. I heartily endorse none of them. Waste of money.
I normally think golf aids are a waste of money or don't work
gimmicks ...a teacher will help more ..and practice!!!!!
Have tried several programs with minimal results. 'May' look into this.

counter_narrative.claim: Traditional speed training DOES increase clubhead speed - the problem is it doesn't help you HIT THE BALL BETTER
counter_narrative.proof: Studies show overspeed training can add 5-10 mph, but golfers report 'I gained 15 mph but my slice got worse' and 'distance on the course is the same'. The speed gains are real - the TRANSFER isn't.
counter_narrative.benefit_transition: SpeedTrac is different because it trains speed AND face control together - so the gains you make are gains you can ACTUALLY USE on the course.

quote_evidence: It definitely works... However, in my case, I became flippy. I developed way too much flip/roll release to generate the speed. So I stopped the training bc my accuracy fell off badly.
I've tried SuperSpeed, Stack, and Rypstick. Gained speed on all of them but my scores haven't changed.

objections.objection: I'll just hit it farther into the woods
how_they_express_it: I gained 15 mph with SuperSpeed but my slice got worse. Now I'm hitting it farther into the trees than before.
Started practicing with speed stik got speed up to 100 mph. But starting spraying ball left, right, and everywhere.
Why can't I take a full swing on the driver without slicing? The harder I swing the more it slices.
Every speed training session without face control is literally practice making your bad habits permanent at higher speeds.
Used the SuperSpeed...my bad contacts also increase dramatically. By the end of day, my score suffers.
For all you guys using speed training tools, does it readily convert to the course?...when I stand on the tee with the risk of a bad outcome, I am more focussed and feel I swing at maybe 85-90%.

counter_narrative.claim: This fear is 100% VALID with traditional speed trainers - because they train speed in isolation without face awareness
counter_narrative.proof: Traditional speed training uses on-axis weight positioning with no feedback on clubface. You literally practice swinging faster with your EXISTING bad habits - so yes, your slice WILL get worse.
counter_narrative.benefit_transition: SpeedTrac solves this by giving you real-time face control feedback during every swing. You're training your brain to swing fast AND square simultaneously - so the speed you build comes with the accuracy to use it.

quote_evidence: I gained 15 mph with SuperSpeed but my slice got worse. Now I'm hitting it farther into the trees than before.
The harder I swing the more it slices. I feel i'm leaving some distance by slowing the swing.
My driving distance on the course is the same. Radar says I gained speed but the ball doesn't go any farther.

objections.objection: Too expensive / I can just swing a weighted club
how_they_express_it: Does anyone have a good speed training program that is cheaper than Stack/Speedsticks? Stack system seems great but is just a bit pricy and I spend enough on this game already.
I don't think one is better than the other. I also think you can make the same gains with a DIY version and doing 20 fast swings twice a week.
The Stack System costs $299 plus $230 for the radar plus $100/year subscription. That's over $600 just to start speed training.
It's an actual club head that you can adjust a considerable amount of weight... What is this going to cost?
Ive had just as good results without any sticks or program, just using common sense.

counter_narrative.claim: The REAL cost of speed training isn't the equipment - it's the wasted rounds hitting farther into trouble
counter_narrative.proof: Stack costs $600+ all-in with radar and subscription. SuperSpeed is $200-250 but requires radar ($150-230). More importantly: golfers report spending $800+ on speed training 'and I'm still hitting it the same distance' or WORSE, 'my slice got worse.'
counter_narrative.benefit_transition: SpeedTrac is one complete system at one price - no radar needed, no subscriptions, no hidden costs. But more importantly, it's the only speed trainer that ensures you can ACTUALLY USE your speed gains instead of just hitting it farther into the woods.

quote_evidence: My wife says I've spent $800 on speed training stuff and I'm still hitting it the same distance. She's not wrong.
The Stack System costs $299 plus $230 for the radar plus $100/year subscription. That's over $600 just to start speed training.
Need to purchase speed measurement device separately. That's another $150-230 on top of the training system.

objections.objection: I'm too old / It won't work at my age
how_they_express_it: I'm 70, still in decent shape, but my swing speeds are diminishing.
I am 71 years old and have lost a lot of distance
Life happens. I held a 3 handicap into my mid-sixties. After that it slowly became a battle to score (and maintain distance) like prior years.
I'm 55 years old and afraid I'm going to hurt myself swinging these sticks as fast as I can.
As an 83 year old golfer most golfing advise found on the internet is useless to a senior golfer.
We all get old. Look at the Senior Tour. You think those guys are hitting it like they used to?

counter_narrative.claim: Age-related distance loss is NOT inevitable - and seniors often see BIGGER gains from proper speed training
counter_narrative.proof: Research shows seniors CAN increase clubhead speed. One golfer in his early 50s added nearly 20 mph. A 62-year-old needing a knee replacement gained 6 mph and 13 yards. A 68-year-old reported 'hitting it farther than I did at 30, 40, and 50 years of age.'
counter_narrative.benefit_transition: SpeedTrac is IDEAL for senior golfers because the face control feedback naturally encourages controlled acceleration - not reckless swinging. You build speed safely while maintaining accuracy. No injury risk from swinging wildly.

quote_evidence: I've had one of my mental golf coaching students get started with Rypstick in his early 50s and added nearly 20 mph to his swing! Proving that you're never too old to get started.

For a 62-year-old needing a knee replacement and losing yardage every year, Rypstick has exceeded my expectations. After five sessions and five rounds, I've increased my swing speed by six mph and added 13 yards to my average drive while also hitting more fairways.
my clubhead speed is up 6-8 mph through the bag. I'm hitting it farther at 68 than I did at 30, 40, and 50 years of age.
Experienced golfers over 55 years of age are shocked they're now hitting the ball farther and straighter than in their 30s and 40s and leaving everybody in their group amazed.
I picked up golf again after almost a 20-year layoff and am playing better and swinging faster than I did at 25.

objections.objection: I need to fix my swing first before working on speed
how_they_express_it: A teacher - Best swing aid there is
but i think some lessons would be a better way to go
If the root cause of your swing issue is not corrected, the device that's designed to correct the reaction is not enough
Before you can utilize any golf training aid that attempts to help you hit the ball you must first prepare your body with general conditioning.

counter_narrative.claim: Traditional advice says fix your swing THEN add speed - but this creates a false choice
counter_narrative.proof: Speed and accuracy aren't separate issues. The REASON your slice gets worse at higher speeds is that traditional speed training doesn't address face control. Fix them together and you solve both problems at once.
counter_narrative.benefit_transition: SpeedTrac trains both simultaneously. As you build speed, you're ALSO building the face awareness that prevents the slice. You don't have to choose between speed and accuracy.

quote_evidence: Swing trainers are designed for a specific purpose often miss the cause of the swing flaw and attempt to re-train the fault itself
The device was just artificially creating the proper path of the putter rather than fixing why that putter had a bad path to begin with

objections.objection: The speed gains won't transfer to the course
how_they_express_it: I've been doing SuperSpeed for 6 months and my driving distance on the course is the same. Radar says I gained speed but the ball doesn't go any farther.
I found that the crossover of benefits of Stack training to my actual on course speeds hit a plateau very quickly.
But has it made a difference to your scoring? Distance is fine but I've yet to see it on a scorecard
The speed sticks are shorter than a driver at 42 inches vs 45 inches. It's closer to 7-iron length. How is that supposed to transfer?

counter_narrative.claim: Transfer failure is the #1 problem with traditional speed training - and there's a specific reason why
counter_narrative.proof: When you stand on the first tee with trees on both sides, your brain won't let you swing as fast as you trained because it knows you'll hit it sideways. The speed is there - the CONFIDENCE to use it isn't.
counter_narrative.benefit_transition: SpeedTrac solves transfer by building confidence along with speed. When you've practiced swinging fast AND square, your brain trusts the movement. The speed comes to the course because the accuracy comes with it.

quote_evidence: when I stand on the tee with the risk of a bad outcome, I am more focussed and feel I swing at maybe 85-90%.
I could not seem to progress beyond those initial gains when I was actually playing golf, despite over two years of pretty consistent Stack training
I've tried SuperSpeed, Stack, and Rypstick. Gained speed on all of them but my scores haven't changed.
